recipes.title: Industrial Mission-Control
recipes.summary: A premium enterprise landing-page formula for industrial, logistics, infrastructure, defense, operations, and AI-control products where one physical world becomes an intelligent command layer across scroll.
use_when: The page sells a serious operational system to enterprise buyers.
The product domain has physical-world stakes such as yards, fleets, facilities, factories, cities, energy, security, logistics, or infrastructure.
The user asks for Terminal Industries, Palantir, command center, mission control, cinematic industrial, or premium operational SaaS energy.
The page needs a hero scroll animation that carries the narrative instead of a static hero.
avoid_when: The target is a repeated-use app screen, settings page, CRUD workflow, or dashboard.
The product is playful, consumer-social, creator-led, or intentionally soft and friendly.
The site has no credible world, object, workflow, or proof surface to carry the cinematic treatment.

reference_examples.label: Terminal Industries
reference_examples.why_it_matters: Uses a scroll-carried logistics world, pre-rendered media, frame sequences, mission-control copy, and proof-heavy enterprise landing structure.

reference_examples.label: Nomous Agency Landing Page
reference_examples.why_it_matters: Private prototype with sticky scroll phases, video scrubbing, generated art layers, readiness checks, and debug-minded cinematic implementation patterns.

top: Open with a full-viewport real world, object, or operation signal rather than a generic software mockup.
Show nav and primary CTA immediately; the cinematic page should still convert.
Name the category or literal offer in the first viewport or first scroll beat.
Let one carrier object or world dominate: yard, fleet, terminal, facility, bridge, map, city, machine, command center, model, or network.
middle: Move from raw physical world to rupture, manual chaos, or hidden inefficiency.
Use the scroll transition to reveal the command layer, model, operating system, map, or AI mission state.
Show three concrete missions or jobs the system performs; each should map to an inspectable visual state.
Use technical labels, numbered markers, grids, overlays, and state changes as product evidence rather than decoration.
proof: Introduce logos, operator quotes, throughput metrics, deployments, case studies, or before/after evidence before the final CTA.
Keep proof visually calmer than the hero so claims are inspectable.
Prefer concrete operational outcomes over generic innovation language.
cta: Return to a stable final command state, product category, or transformed world.
Repeat one clear CTA with an enterprise buyer verb such as schedule, talk, see, evaluate, or deploy.
Avoid ending on only atmosphere; the final screen should make the next action obvious.

required: desktop hero video or frame sequence
mobile hero video or frame sequence
recommended: 3-6 second support loops for feature sections
generated fallback stills for sections without real product media
debug HUD for progress, phase, frame, and readiness
keep_out_of_generated_video: UI labels that need responsive layout

qa_expectations: First viewport clearly signals product category or physical domain.
Desktop and mobile checkpoints show distinct story phases.
All video, poster, frame, and generated asset URLs return 200.
Reduced-motion mode renders a strong static version.
Text is HTML-rendered, responsive, and does not overlap at mobile widths.
Review includes source-level frontend guidelines and taste scoring when code changes.
